scenario: You need to cut a complex shape out of another complex shape.

comparison.Boolean SOP: Uses pure polygon math. Pros: Preserves original topology, precise sharp edges, keeps UVs intact. Cons: Will fail completely if inputs have non-manifold geometry, holes, or perfectly overlapping coplanar faces.
comparison.VDB Boolean: Converts meshes to volumes, does volumetric math, and converts back. Pros: 100% robust, never fails on messy/intersecting geometry. Cons: Destroys original topology, loses UVs, can soften sharp edges.

recommendation: Use Boolean SOP for hard-surface mechanical modeling where precise edges and UVs are required. Use VDB Boolean for organic shapes, 3D printing preparation, or when dealing with incredibly messy proxy geometry.

scenario: You need to scatter 50,000 trees on a terrain.

comparison.Copy To Points (Raw): Duplicates the actual polygons 50,000 times. Will freeze Houdini and crash your RAM.
comparison.Copy To Points (Packed): Creates 50,000 pointers to a single mesh. Highly efficient in the viewport, uses minimal RAM.
comparison.Instancing (Render Time): Points exist in the viewport, geometry is only generated during rendering (Mantra/Karma/Redshift).

recommendation: Always use 'Pack and Instance' on the Copy To Points SOP for asset generation. Use pure point instancing for environments exceeding millions of elements.

scenario: You need to set a 'pscale' attribute and initialize velocity '@v'.

comparison.Attribute Create: UI-based. Good for setting a single constant value. Cumbersome for math or multiple attributes.
comparison.Attribute Wrangle: VEX-based. Runs multithreaded. You can type `f@pscale = 1.0; v@v = set(0,1,0);` in one line.

recommendation: Learn basic VEX and use Attribute Wrangle. It is vastly faster, cleaner to read, and infinitely more flexible than Attribute Create.

scenario: You need to alter the polygon density of a mesh.

comparison.Subdivide: Smooths the mesh and quadruples the polygon count using Catmull-Clark math. Preserves edge loops.
comparison.Remesh: Destroys topology to create a perfectly uniform web of equilateral triangles. Ideal for simulations (Cloth/RBD).
comparison.PolyReduce: Intelligently collapses edges to lower polygon count while trying to preserve the silhouette. Destroys loops.

recommendation: Use Subdivide for high-end rendering. Use Remesh to prep geometry for physics simulations. Use PolyReduce for game engine LODs.

topic: Group Bounding Box vs Group Create (Topology)
scenario: You need to select the top half of an object to apply a material.

comparison.Group Bounding Box: Selects anything inside a 3D volume. If the object deforms or moves, the selection changes dynamically.
comparison.Group Create (Normals/Edges): Selects based on geometric rules (e.g., 'faces pointing UP'). Moves with the object.

recommendation: Use Bounding Box for spatial effects (like cutting off the bottom of a generated building). Use Topology rules for procedural shading (like putting snow only on upward-facing polygons).

topic: When to use VEX Wrangle vs VOP network
scenario: You need to write custom logic to modify point positions or attributes.

comparison.VEX Wrangle (Code): Requires typing code. Pros: Extremely fast to write, highly compact, easy to copy/paste between nodes or forums. Cons: Requires knowing VEX syntax and functions.
comparison.VOP Network (Nodes): Visual programming. Pros: Easy to discover functions by hitting TAB, great for complex noise blending, no syntax errors. Cons: Sprawling networks become unreadable spaghetti quickly; slower to build.

recommendation: Use VEX Wrangles for math, conditionals (if/else), and simple attribute assignments. Use VOPs strictly for building complex layered Noise, as visual blending of noise outputs is much more intuitive.

scenario: You have multiple streams of geometry and need to combine or select them.

comparison.Merge: Takes all inputs and puts them in the same space simultaneously. Point/primitive numbers are concatenated.
comparison.Switch: Only outputs ONE of the inputs at a time, based on an integer parameter. Often driven by expressions or UI toggles.
comparison.Blend: Takes two inputs with EXACTLY the same point count/topology, and interpolates point positions between them (morphing).

recommendation: Use Merge to build composite assets. Use Switch for logic/variations (e.g. LODs, or choosing between a square or round table). Use Blend for shape-key style animation/morphing.

scenario: You are manipulating a Curve and need to alter its structure.

comparison.Resample: Rebuilds the curve with evenly spaced points based on a length parameter. Essential for smooth deformation.
comparison.Carve: Slices the curve based on a 0-1 percentage. Acts like animating a line drawing on or off. Does not change point spacing.
comparison.Ends: Used specifically to close an open curve (make it a loop) or open a closed curve. Can also unroll polygons into curves.

recommendation: Use Resample immediately after drawing any curve to ensure stable topology. Use Carve to animate paths growing. Use Ends to close loops before sweeping/extruding.

comparison.Vellum: Position-based dynamics. Best for cloth, hair, strings, grains, and squishy soft bodies. Fast on GPU.
comparison.RBD (Bullet): Rigid body dynamics. Best for breaking glass, crumbling concrete, rigid metal collisions. Does not bend.
comparison.FEM: Finite Element Method. Extremely slow, highly accurate. Used for hyper-realistic muscle/tissue simulations in creature FX.

recommendation: Use RBD for destruction. Use Vellum for almost everything else (cloth, squishy things). Ignore FEM unless doing advanced character FX.

scenario: You are building a complex procedural asset (like a cavern or a spaceship hull).

comparison.VDB (Volumes): Pros: Never fails booleans, smooths intersections beautifully, completely procedural. Cons: High memory, loses sharp edges, destroys UVs.
comparison.Polygon (Standard): Pros: Fast for simple shapes, keeps UVs, perfect sharp edges. Cons: Booleans fail easily, topology gets messy.

recommendation: Use Polygons for hard surface/mechanical assets where sharp edges and UVs matter. Use VDB for organic assets (rocks, terrain, fluid meshes) where topology just needs to be contiguous.

scenario: You are duplicating an object thousands of times using Copy to Points.

comparison.Pack: Creates a packed primitive in memory. You still 'copy' that packed pointer. Good for standard scenes.
comparison.Pack and Instance: Tells the renderer (Mantra/Karma) to only load the geometry once and draw it X times dynamically. Viewport only shows points or bounding boxes.

recommendation: Always enable 'Pack and Instance' on the CopyToPoints SOP if you have more than 10,000 copies, or if the source geometry is extremely dense.

topic: Point vs Primitive attributes for Shading
scenario: You want to assign a color (@Cd) to your geometry.

comparison.Point Attributes: Stored on the vertices. Creates a smooth gradient between points. (e.g. Red point next to Blue point creates a purple gradient face).
comparison.Primitive Attributes: Stored on the face. Gives the entire polygon one solid, flat color. No gradients.

recommendation: Use Point attributes for organic, smooth color blending. Use Primitive attributes for hard, low-poly, or distinct material assignments.

topic: Heightfield vs Polygon Grid for Terrain
scenario: You need to build a mountain range.

comparison.Heightfield (2D Volume): Incredibly fast. Includes dedicated erosion, terracing, and noise nodes. Used by game engines directly.
comparison.Polygon Grid + Mountain: Slow. Real 3D geometry. Can have overhangs and caves (Heightfields cannot).

recommendation: Always use Heightfields for large-scale landscapes and terrain generation. Only convert to Polygons at the very end if needed for rendering or specific collisions.
